author: @edwardcdg
text:
Brazed aluminum is said to be stronger than TIG welded.  Welding melts the base metal to fuse it together so it loses it's temper and thus some strength which it never regains.  Brazing, as you say, is more like soldering.  The brazing is as strong  as the base metal, so no loss.  
I ran across this stuff a while back and never got it to work for me.  Probably operator error plus poor choice of torch and supporting materials.  Thanks for the tips!

author: @LowSpecLinuxLaptop

author: @brianday6433
text: I've been using those aluminum rods for a few years. It can be a bit aggravating at times if you have a thicker part to do. If you can get the heat right, and it's clean, it will flow great. Heat the part, not the rod.

author: @William_Hada
text: Great stuff! I think the rubbing probably scrapes away aluminum oxide from the surface. When cleaning the surface you think you have cleaned off all oxide but aluminum oxide reforms almost instantaneously in air.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
Actually, when I solder, I always keep the flame off of the joints as I can, pre-heating it then rubbing on the solder, it gives a cleaner job with less mess and usually bonds more rapidly in my experience.

I would say that in most applications, especially those using a flame, one indeed SHOULD solder the same way that you were applying this aluminum brazing/soldering material. 
~( 'w')/

author: @em9594
text:
Chemalloy was created by Samuel Freedman in 1951,he mad it to braze aluminum together, he also found that fine powdered chemalloy could actually break apart water in to oxygen and hydrogen with no added electricity, each partial was able to generate a couple micro volts of electricity and when you add up all that powder you had thousand of volts! I couldn't source the materials to make the stuff myself but you folks might want to investigate it! 
A little info and e recipe:
http://chemalloy.blogspot.com/2012/01/chemalloy-samuel-freedman.html?m=1

author: @Telectronics
text: Just to point out in case somebody tries that and the solder doesn´t want to stick. I found out that the hardwarestore aluminium often has an anodising coated on it ! So don´t forget to remove it or you will wonder why this aluminium won´t braze even with name brand rods ;) To remove the coating you can use sodium hydroxide in water 1-5% or the belt grinder for that.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
(name brand) Durafix is definitely the go-to stuff for alloy brazing.  That said, it's not the best solution in many cases, and you're liable to be disappointed by eventual strength, although it's far better than most of the cheap "clones".  It's closer to soft solder or a well-prepared epoxy join than braze in terms of strength.  Aluminium's a bastard to get to stick properly, the whole thing about abrading is to do with getting rid of the oxide layer that forms spontaneously. Prep is everything, even with stuff like the durafix rods.   Actual welding of aluminium is really hard to do well, though, and needs relatively expensive equipment, so durafix definitely fills a niche.  If you're having to rub the stuff in to get it to flow, you're probably not quite hot enough, or you haven't managed to get shut of all the oxide.

For your axle bond, you'd probably be far better off with one of loctite's products, something like 648 retaining compound on a well-fitting cylindrical joint will be far stronger than anything you can do with durafix or much anything else.  For flat to flat joins with a decent surface area and close fitting, any old supermarket no-name cyanoacrylate "super glue" will do the trick.  I've never tried retaining compound on flat bonds, I probably should (the stuff I have doesn't come from Henkel, but it produces equivalent bonds on cylindrical features).  For on-edge joining like your involute, of course, a fillet braze is pretty much what the doctor ordered, although it might be easier to simply clamp between the two outer faces - brazing into a 90 degree edge is tricky, as you found, much kudos for getting any sort of decent result.  I'd have been very tempted to change the design in order to be able to use adhesives, but playing with something new is always a learning experience so hey, why not.

The idea of the stainless brush, btw, is that it doesn't contaminate the surface.  As long as you never use it to brush anything but aluminium, clean all grease and crap off the surface first, don't fart near it if you've had  curry, etc etc.  Clean, fine grit abrasive is fine as an alternative.

author: @benthere8051
text:
I watched several of the YT videos on aluminum brazing before I bought my rods. I was very impressed with the flow characteristics and the fillet I got between the two pieces being joined. It seemed to have a lot of the character of a good weld.
I got a distinct impression that a non-stainless steel brush would generate problems with residue. Your use of sandpaper would avoid that. I am also impressed with its ability to bridge large gaps and fill holes. I have yet to try that.
Getting a good joint at 700 F puts the technology within reach of a propane torch which I like as well.

author: @NorthernKitty
text: The more I watch your channel... the more I realize, "I need a bigger workshop!!"  😄  Actually think I need to slow down the "making" a little and start working on organizing space and getting rid of clutter. replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like cleaning up and organising my shop - it does the same thing to my mind as I clean the space around me

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I have a very bad "perfectionist" habit that paralyzes me when I'm not satisfied with a solution, which is how things get so cluttered! 😊  It's no exaggeration, I can sit staring for two hours trying to decide where the best spot is to store my hot glue gun... and STILL end up not finding a place.  I can't explain it - I efficiently directed others on complex projects for many years, but when it comes to my own "stuff" I struggle with the simplest decisions.  If I were my own boss, I'd have fired me by now. 😋

author: @littleworkshopofhorrors2395
text:
Some of those style solders need to be scratched through the molten bead with a pointy stainless steel rod to break through the oxide layer for better adhesion. 
They are great for fixing machining cock ups in alloy parts, saved me hours of remanufacturing time more than once.

author: @borisk6969
text: Just a quick note, the significance of the little wire brush, is that its a stainless steel brush. Experience has taught me that A mild steel wire brush or wire wool can contaminate the braze. Cheers

author: @Neutral777
text: Aluminim can be soldered with ususl solder, jusd and some motor oil on aluminum ant scrach it under oil. Oil prevents aluminum from oxidation, then with soldering iron heat that scrached place and add solder. Solder  realy bonds very well to aluminum
